What Can and Cannot Be Seeded\n\n### CAN Be Seeded (Pre-populated Before Task Starts)\n| Type | How | Notes |\n|------|-----|-------|\n| **Emails** | `seed_emails` in task JSON | Appears in agent's inbox |\n| **Calendar Events** | `seed_calendar_events` in task JSON | Events on NPC calendars |\n| **Chat Channels** | `seed_group_channels` in task JSON | Pre-existing channel membership + history context |\n| **NPC Secrets** | `npcs[].secret` in task JSON | Info NPCs reveal when asked via Chat |\n| **HRIS Data** | Frappe seed files | Employee records, candidates, requisitions |\n\n### CANNOT Be Seeded\n| Type | Why | Alternative |\n|------|-----|-------------|\n| **DM History** | Direct-message threads cannot be pre-seeded | Use `seed_emails`, NPC secrets, or seeded channels |\n\n### Task Design Implications\n\n**Tasks must NOT assume:**\n- Existing DM history the agent can read at task start\n- Ability for the agent to create channels/groups\n\n**Tasks MAY assume (when seeded):**\n- Existing channel context/messages in `seed_group_channels`\n- Use of explicitly named channels already present in seed data\n\n**If task needs info from someone:**\n1. Use `seed_emails` (email from the NPC forwarding relevant info)\n2. Add an NPC `secret` (agent asks via Chat, NPC replies)\n3. Include directly in task description\n\n### Multi-Person Decisions\n\nFor panel interviews, debriefs, or group decisions:\n- **Each decision-maker should have their own NPC secret**\n- Secrets should reflect individual perspectives/feedback\n- This ensures agent must consult each relevant stakeholder\n\nExample for 3-person interview panel:\n```json\n{\n  \"npcs\": [\n    {\"id\": \"michael_chen\", \"secret\": \"I thought the candidate was strong on system design but needs more depth on algorithms.\"},\n    {\"id\": \"emily_davis\", \"secret\": \"Good culture fit, but I have concerns about their experience with our scale.\"},\n    {\"id\": \"sarah_johnson\", \"secret\": \"I'd recommend a hire - their leadership experience outweighs the technical gaps.\"}\n  ]\n}\n```\n"}, {"name": "accommodation_request_sop", "title": "Accommodation Request SOP", "content": "# Amazon People Management\n## Accommodation Request SOP\n\nThis SOP covers workplace accommodation requests (including accessibility needs, schedule flexibility, and return-to-work adjustments).\n\n### Scope\n- Applies to employees and contractors.\n- Covers disability accommodations, temporary medical accommodations, and ergonomic needs.\n- Does not cover immigration/legal accommodations.\n\n### Intake Channels\n- Employee requests via HR chat/email\n- Manager requests on behalf of employee (with employee consent)\n- HRBP/ER referrals\n\n### Required Intake Information\n- Employee name, department, manager\n- Requested accommodation (what is needed)\n- Duration (temporary vs ongoing)\n- Work impact (what tasks/meetings are affected)\n- Documentation status:\n  - if medical documentation is required, request only what is necessary\n  - store documentation separately from general HR notes\n\n### Triage and Ownership\n- Default owner: HRBP for the employee\u2019s department\n- Escalate to ER if:\n  - there is an active conflict, disciplinary action, or investigation\n  - the request is tied to a complaint (harassment/retaliation)\n- Escalate to Legal if:\n  - litigation threat, formal legal complaint, or high-risk precedent\n\n### Decision and Approval\n- HRBP reviews feasibility with manager\n- If cost/equipment is required, coordinate with IT/Facilities per equipment process\n- Approval outcomes:\n  - Approved as requested\n  - Approved with modification (document rationale)\n  - Denied (document objective reason; offer alternatives where possible)\n\n### Implementation Steps\n1. Confirm approved accommodation details in writing (email recap).\n2. Update HRIS notes with a non-medical summary (need-to-know only).\n3. If scheduling changes are needed:\n   - update recurring meetings, focus blocks, or on-call coverage planning\n4. If equipment is needed:\n   - submit equipment request and track fulfillment\n\n### Privacy Rules\n- Do not include diagnosis or detailed medical info in calendar invites or broad emails.\n- Share only what is necessary for implementation.\n\n### Time Expectations\n- Acknowledge receipt: within 1 business day\n- First triage/clarification: within 3 business days\n- Decision target: within 10 business days (or provide an ETA)\n\n"}, {"name": "benefits_enrollment", "title": "BENEFITS ENROLLMENT GUIDE", "content": "# AMAZON\n## BENEFITS ENROLLMENT GUIDE\n\n**Document Number:** APM-BEN-001  \n**Effective Date:** January 1, 2024  \n**Plan Year:** 2026  \n**Benefits Department:** (555) 200-4500 | benefits@amazon.com\n\n---\n\n## 1. WELCOME TO YOUR BENEFITS\n\nAmazon provides a comprehensive benefits package for your health, financial security, and work-life balance. This guide helps you understand your options and complete enrollment.\n\n### 1.1 Important Dates\n\n**New Hire Enrollment:**\n- Eligibility begins: First day of the month following 30 days of employment\n- Enrollment deadline: Within 30 days of hire date\n- Coverage effective date: First day of month following enrollment\n\n**Annual Open Enrollment:**\n- Enrollment period: November 1-30 (prior year)\n- Coverage effective date: January 1\n- Changes apply for the full plan year unless you have a qualifying life event\n\n**Qualifying Life Events (QLE):**\n- You have 31 days from the QLE date to make changes\n- Examples: marriage, divorce, birth/adoption, loss of other coverage, change in employment status\n\n### 1.2 Who Is Eligible\n\n**Employees:**\n- Full-time employees working 30+ hours per week\n- Part-time employees working 20+ hours per week (limited benefits)\n\n**Dependents:**\n- Legal spouse or domestic partner\n- Children up to age 26\n- Disabled dependents over age 26 (with documentation)\n\n### 1.3 How to Enroll\n\n**Online Enrollment:**\n1. Log into the benefits portal (link in email)\n2. Use your employee ID and temporary password\n3. Review benefit options and costs\n4. Make your elections and submit by the deadline\n\n**Need Help?**\n- Benefits Service Center: (555) 200-4500\n- Email: benefits@amazon.com\n- Rocket.Chat: #Benefits-Questions or HR-Support\n\n## 2. MEDICAL INSURANCE\n\nAmazon offers medical coverage through multiple carriers including Premera Blue Cross, UnitedHealthcare, and Aetna. Plan options include PPO and HDHP.\n\n### 2.1 Plan Tiers\n\n- **Employee Only** \u2014 Individual coverage\n- **Employee + 1** \u2014 Employee and one dependent\n- **Family** \u2014 Employee and all eligible dependents\n\n### 2.2 Coverage and Cost Sharing\n\nDetails for each plan (deductibles, copays, out-of-pocket limits) are in the plan comparison tool on the benefits portal. Contact the Benefits team (Rachel Green, James Liu) or use #Benefits-Questions for plan-specific questions.\n\n## 3. OTHER BENEFITS\n\n- **Dental & Vision** \u2014 Available during open enrollment or with a QLE\n- **401(k)** \u2014 Company match; enroll or update via the benefits portal\n- **Leave** \u2014 PTO, parental leave, and other leave types per Amazon policy\n\nFor questions, contact the People team via Rocket.Chat (HR-Support, Benefits-Questions) or benefits@amazon.com.\n"}, {"name": "candidate_faq", "title": "Compensation & Equity", "content": "# Candidate FAQ\n\n## Compensation & Equity\n\n### How is base salary determined?\nBase salary is set based on role, level, location, and experience. Each level has a defined range (min/mid/max). Most offers start at or near the midpoint.\n\n### How do RSUs work?\nRSUs vest over 4 years with a 1-year cliff:\n- Year 1: 5% (at 12-month anniversary)\n- Year 2: 15% (quarterly vesting)\n- Years 3-4: 40% each year (quarterly vesting)\n\nRSUs are taxed as ordinary income when they vest.\n\n### Is the sign-on bonus negotiable?\nSign-on bonuses are used to bridge the gap when candidates have unvested equity at their current company. We can discuss your specific situation.\n\n### What about annual raises and bonuses?\n- Annual merit increases: typically 3-8% based on performance\n- Annual bonus target varies by level, paid in Q1\n- Equity refresh grants: awarded annually based on performance\n\n## Benefits\n\n### When do benefits start?\nBenefits effective dates vary by program. Some benefits may begin on your first day, while others may have eligibility requirements or waiting periods (see the relevant plan documents).\n\n### What health insurance options are available?\nWe offer medical coverage options. For the authoritative plan details (deductibles, copays, out-of-pocket max, network rules), see `health_insurance_plan.md`.\n\n### Is the PTO really unlimited?\nYes, we have an unlimited discretionary PTO policy. We encourage at least 2 weeks per year. Manager approval is required but rarely denied for reasonable requests.\n\n### What's the 401(k) match?\nSee `401K_policy.md` for eligibility, match formula, and vesting details.\n\n## Work Arrangements\n\n### What does hybrid mean?\nOur hybrid model requires 3 days per week in the office (typically Tue-Thu). 2 days can be worked remotely. Core collaboration hours are 10am-3pm local time.\n\n### Can I work fully remote?\nFully remote arrangements are considered case-by-case and depend on role requirements. Discuss with your hiring manager if this is important to you.\n\n### What about relocation?\n- **Domestic moves**: $10,000 lump sum\n- **International moves**: $25,000 lump sum + visa sponsorship\n- **Temporary housing**: 30 days covered for domestic moves\n\n## Timeline & Process\n\n### When will I hear back after my interview?\nWe aim to make decisions within 5 business days of your final interview. Your recruiter will reach out with next steps.\n\n### How long is the offer valid?\nOffers typically expire within 3-5 business days. If you need more time, please discuss with your recruiter.\n\n### What happens after I accept?\n1. Background check initiated (typically 3-5 business days)\n2. I-9 verification and new hire paperwork\n3. Laptop and equipment shipped before start date\n4. Welcome email with Day 1 logistics\n\n### Can I negotiate my start date?\nYes, start dates are flexible within reason. Most candidates start on a Monday. We typically can accommodate 2-4 weeks from offer acceptance.\n\n## Team & Role\n\n### What's the onboarding like?\n- Week 1: Orientation, compliance training, team introductions\n- Weeks 2-4: Role-specific training, 1:1s with stakeholders\n- First 90 days: Defined onboarding projects with clear success metrics\n\n## Visa & Work Authorization\n\n### Do you sponsor visas?\nYes, we sponsor H-1B, L-1, and green cards for eligible roles. Discuss your specific situation with your recruiter.\n\n### I'm on OPT/STEM OPT\u2014is that okay?\nYes, we can hire candidates on OPT or STEM OPT and sponsor H-1B before the authorization expires.\n"}, {"name": "compliance_training_calendar", "title": "Compliance Training Calendar", "content": "# Amazon People Management\n## Compliance Training Calendar\n\nThis calendar defines mandatory trainings, deadlines, and escalation rules.\n\n### Annual Mandatory Trainings (All Employees)\n| Course Code | Course Name | Frequency | Deadline |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| COC-101 | Code of Conduct | Annual | Jan 31 |\n| HAR-201 | Anti-Harassment & Discrimination | Annual | Jan 31 |\n| SEC-110 | Information Security Awareness | Annual | Jan 31 |\n| PRIV-120 | Data Privacy (GDPR/CCPA) | Annual | Jan 31 |\n\n### Role-Based Mandatory Trainings\n| Role | Course Code | Course Name | Deadline |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| People Managers | MGR-301 | Performance Management Essentials | Within 30 days of becoming a manager |\n| HR / People Team | ER-401 | Investigation Fundamentals | Within 60 days of joining People team |\n| Finance | FIN-210 | Financial Controls & Expense Policy | Mar 31 |\n\n### New Hire Deadlines\n- Complete annual mandatory trainings within 7 calendar days of start date.\n\n### Non-Compliance Escalation\n1. Day 1 after deadline: automated reminder email.\n2. Day 7 after deadline: manager notified.\n3. Day 14 after deadline: HRBP notified; employee required to complete within 7 days.\n4. Day 21 after deadline: ER review for potential disciplinary action.\n\n"}, {"name": "disciplinary_action_policy", "title": "DISCIPLINARY ACTION POLICY", "content": "# WEAVER ENTERPRISES INC.\n## DISCIPLINARY ACTION POLICY\n\n**Document Number:** HR-POL-009  \n**Effective Date:** January 1, 2024  \n**Last Revised:** January 1, 2024  \n**Policy Owner:** Human Resources Department  \n**Approved By:** Chief Human Resources Officer\n\n---\n\n## 1. PURPOSE AND SCOPE\n\nThis policy establishes guidelines for addressing employee misconduct and policy violations at Weaver Enterprises Inc. The purpose is to:\n\n- Correct unacceptable behavior and performance issues\n- Ensure fair and consistent treatment of all employees\n- Maintain a productive and professional work environment\n- Comply with employment laws and regulations\n- Provide employees with clear expectations and opportunities to improve\n- Protect the organization and other employees\n\nThis policy applies to all employees. The Company reserves the right to exercise discretion in determining appropriate disciplinary action based on the specific circumstances of each situation.\n\n## 2. EMPLOYMENT AT-WILL\n\nEmployment at Weaver Enterprises is at-will, meaning either the employee or the Company may terminate the employment relationship at any time, with or without cause or notice.\n\n**This policy does not:**\n- Create a contract of employment\n- Guarantee employment for any specific period\n- Require progressive discipline before termination\n- Limit the Company's right to terminate employment\n\nProgressive discipline is generally followed when appropriate, but the Company reserves the right to skip steps or proceed directly to termination for serious violations.\n\n## 3. GENERAL PRINCIPLES\n\n### 3.1 Consistency and Fairness\n\n- Similar violations should result in similar consequences\n- Discipline should be proportionate to the severity of the offense\n- Individual circumstances may warrant different approaches\n- Documentation is essential for all disciplinary actions\n\n### 3.2 Corrective Purpose\n\nThe goal of discipline is correction, not punishment:\n- Help employees understand expectations\n- Provide opportunity to improve\n- Support employees in meeting standards\n- Separate employment only when necessary\n\n### 3.3 Confidentiality\n\n- Disciplinary matters kept confidential\n- Information shared only on need-to-know basis\n- Employee privacy respected\n- Documentation stored securely in personnel files\n\n### 3.4 Non-Retaliation\n\nEmployees will not face retaliation for:\n- Reporting policy violations in good faith\n- Participating in investigations\n- Exercising legal rights\n- Engaging in protected activities\n\nRetaliation is itself a serious policy violation subject to discipline.\n\n## 4. PROGRESSIVE DISCIPLINE FRAMEWORK\n\n### 4.1 Progressive Discipline Steps\n\nWhen appropriate, the Company follows progressive discipline:\n\n**Step 1: Verbal Warning**\n- Informal discussion of concern\n- Explanation of expected behavior\n- Documentation in manager's notes\n- No formal documentation in personnel file\n\n**Step 2: Written Warning**\n- Formal written documentation\n- Specific description of violation\n- Expected corrective action\n- Timeline for improvement\n- Consequences of continued violations\n- Placed in personnel file\n\n**Step 3: Final Written Warning**\n- Formal documentation of continued or serious violation\n- Clear statement that employment is in jeopardy\n- Specific expectations and timeline\n- Notification that next violation may result in termination\n- Placed in personnel file\n\n**Step 4: Suspension (with or without pay)**\n- Employee removed from workplace temporarily\n- Allows for investigation or cooling-off period\n- May be paid or unpaid depending on circumstances\n- Duration typically 1-5 days\n- Return-to-work conditions specified\n\n**Step 5: Termination**\n- Employment relationship ended\n- Final step in progressive discipline\n- Or immediate action for serious violations\n\n### 4.2 When Progressive Discipline Applies\n\nProgressive discipline is generally appropriate for:\n- Attendance and punctuality issues\n- Minor policy violations\n- Work quality or productivity concerns (when not addressed through performance management)\n- First-time minor infractions\n- Situations where employee can reasonably improve\n\n### 4.3 When Progressive Discipline May Be Skipped\n\nThe Company may bypass progressive discipline and proceed directly to suspension or termination for:\n- Serious misconduct or policy violations\n- Illegal activity\n- Behavior that threatens safety or security\n- Gross insubordination\n- Theft, fraud, or dishonesty\n- Violence or threats of violence\n- Harassment or discrimination\n- Breach of confidentiality\n- Intoxication or drug use at work\n- Willful destruction of Company property\n- Conduct causing significant harm to Company or employees\n\n## 5. COMMON VIOLATIONS AND POTENTIAL CONSEQUENCES\n\n### 5.1 Attendance and Punctuality\n\n**Examples:**\n- Excessive absenteeism\n- Chronic tardiness\n- Leaving work early without permission\n- No-call, no-show\n- Abuse of break or lunch periods\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- 1st occurrence: Verbal warning\n- 2nd occurrence: Written warning\n- 3rd occurrence: Final written warning\n- 4th occurrence: Termination\n\n**Exception:** No-call, no-show for 3 consecutive days may result in immediate termination for job abandonment.\n\n### 5.2 Performance Issues\n\n**Examples:**\n- Failure to meet job requirements\n- Substandard work quality\n- Lack of productivity\n- Failure to follow instructions\n\n**Note:** Performance issues are generally addressed through performance management process (see Performance Management Policy), not disciplinary action. Discipline appropriate when performance issues involve:\n- Willful failure to perform duties\n- Refusal to follow reasonable instructions\n- Negligent or careless work causing harm\n\n### 5.3 Policy Violations\n\n**Examples:**\n- Dress code violations\n- Inappropriate use of technology\n- Smoking in prohibited areas\n- Failure to follow safety procedures\n- Unauthorized overtime\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- 1st occurrence: Verbal or written warning\n- 2nd occurrence: Written or final written warning\n- 3rd occurrence: Suspension or termination\n\nSeverity of consequence depends on nature and impact of violation.\n\n### 5.4 Insubordination\n\n**Examples:**\n- Refusal to follow reasonable work directive\n- Disrespectful behavior toward supervisor\n- Undermining manager's authority\n- Refusing assigned tasks without legitimate reason\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- Minor insubordination: Written warning\n- Serious insubordination: Final written warning or suspension\n- Gross insubordination: Immediate termination\n\n### 5.5 Conduct and Behavior\n\n**Examples:**\n- Unprofessional behavior\n- Rudeness to coworkers, customers, or vendors\n- Gossip or spreading rumors\n- Inappropriate language\n- Creating hostile work environment\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- 1st occurrence: Written warning\n- 2nd occurrence: Final written warning or suspension\n- 3rd occurrence: Termination\n\nHarassment, discrimination, or bullying may result in immediate termination (see Section 5.7).\n\n### 5.6 Dishonesty and Integrity\n\n**Examples:**\n- Falsifying records or documents\n- Lying to supervisor or during investigation\n- Time theft (falsifying time records)\n- Providing false information on application or resume\n- Theft of Company or employee property\n- Fraud or embezzlement\n- Unauthorized disclosure of confidential information\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- Immediate termination for most violations\n- Investigation and suspension pending investigation\n- May involve law enforcement referral\n\n### 5.7 Harassment, Discrimination, and Retaliation\n\n**Examples:**\n- Sexual harassment\n- Harassment based on protected characteristics\n- Discrimination in any form\n- Retaliation against someone who reports concerns\n- Creating hostile work environment\n- Bullying or intimidating behavior\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- Immediate suspension pending investigation\n- Termination if substantiated\n- Mandatory training if less severe (rare)\n\nZero tolerance for harassment, discrimination, and retaliation.\n\n### 5.8 Safety Violations\n\n**Examples:**\n- Failure to follow safety procedures\n- Not wearing required personal protective equipment\n- Creating unsafe conditions\n- Failure to report accidents or injuries\n- Operating equipment without authorization\n- Horseplay or reckless behavior\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- Minor safety violation: Written warning\n- Serious safety violation: Final written warning or suspension\n- Egregious safety violation: Immediate termination\n\n### 5.9 Substance Abuse\n\n**Examples:**\n- Reporting to work under the influence of alcohol or drugs\n- Using, possessing, or distributing illegal drugs at work\n- Alcohol consumption during work hours (except approved events)\n- Refusing drug/alcohol test when required\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- Immediate suspension pending investigation\n- Termination for confirmed violation\n- May be offered EAP assistance in some circumstances\n\n### 5.10 Technology and Social Media\n\n**Examples:**\n- Accessing inappropriate websites\n- Excessive personal use of company technology\n- Unauthorized disclosure of confidential information online\n- Posting disparaging comments about Company or employees\n- Using Company equipment for personal business\n- Installing unauthorized software\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- Minor violation: Written warning\n- Serious violation: Final written warning or termination\n- Breach of confidentiality: Immediate termination\n\n### 5.11 Violence and Threats\n\n**Examples:**\n- Physical violence or assault\n- Threats of violence\n- Intimidating or threatening behavior\n- Possession of weapons on Company property (except where legally authorized)\n- Stalking or menacing behavior\n\n**Typical Discipline:**\n- Immediate suspension or termination\n- Law enforcement involvement\n- Workplace violence threat assessment\n- No tolerance policy\n\n## 6. DISCIPLINARY PROCESS\n\n### 6.1 Investigation\n\nBefore imposing discipline (except verbal warnings):\n- Gather facts about the alleged violation\n- Interview relevant parties and witnesses\n- Review applicable policies and documentation\n- Consider employee's explanation and circumstances\n- Consult with HR\n\n**Investigation principles:**\n- Prompt and thorough\n- Fair and impartial\n- Confidential to extent possible\n- Well-documented\n\n### 6.2 Deciding Appropriate Action\n\nConsider the following factors:\n- Severity of the violation\n- Impact on Company, employees, or customers\n- Employee's disciplinary history\n- Length of service and overall performance\n- Mitigating or aggravating circumstances\n- Consistency with past practice\n- Whether employee knew or should have known rule\n- Intent (willful vs. negligent vs. accidental)\n\n### 6.3 Disciplinary Meeting\n\n**Preparation:**\n- Review all facts and documentation\n- Determine appropriate disciplinary action\n- Prepare disciplinary documentation\n- Consult with HR\n- Schedule private meeting\n\n**Meeting Structure:**\n1. Explain purpose of meeting\n2. Describe the violation with specific facts\n3. Allow employee to respond and provide explanation\n4. Explain disciplinary action and consequences\n5. Outline expectations going forward\n6. Review written documentation with employee\n7. Answer questions\n8. Have employee sign acknowledgment\n\n**Meeting Best Practices:**\n- Remain calm and professional\n- Focus on facts and behavior, not personality\n- Listen to employee's perspective\n- Don't argue or become defensive\n- Be clear and direct\n- End meeting on constructive note when possible\n- Have HR or witness present for serious discipline\n\n### 6.4 Documentation Requirements\n\n**Disciplinary documentation must include:**\n- Employee name and position\n- Date of violation and date of disciplinary action\n- Specific description of violation (who, what, when, where)\n- Which policy or rule was violated\n- Employee's explanation or response\n- Prior disciplinary actions (if any)\n- Disciplinary action taken\n- Expected corrective action\n- Consequences of future violations\n- Signatures (employee and manager)\n\n**Employee Signature:**\n- Signature indicates receipt, not agreement\n- Employee may add written comments\n- If employee refuses to sign, note refusal and have witness sign\n\n**Document Distribution:**\n- Original to personnel file\n- Copy to employee\n- Copy to manager's file\n- Copy to HR\n\n### 6.5 Suspension Pending Investigation\n\n**When Used:**\n- Serious allegations requiring investigation\n- Safety concern or risk to others\n- Potential criminal activity\n- Need to preserve evidence\n- Employee's presence would interfere with investigation\n\n**Process:**\n- Typically paid suspension (unless policy violation merits unpaid)\n- Employee notified verbally and in writing\n- Expected duration communicated\n- Investigation completed promptly\n- Employee notified of outcome\n\n**Possible Outcomes:**\n- Reinstatement with no discipline (if unfounded)\n- Reinstatement with discipline (if substantiated)\n- Termination (if violation warrants)\n\n## 7. TERMINATION OF EMPLOYMENT\n\n### 7.1 Voluntary Termination\n\n**Resignation:**\n- Employee provides notice (2 weeks standard, more for senior roles)\n- Submit written resignation letter\n- Complete exit process\n- Return Company property\n- Final paycheck includes accrued PTO (if eligible per policy)\n\n**Job Abandonment:**\n- No-call, no-show for 3 consecutive days\n- Considered voluntary resignation\n- Employment terminated\n- Documentation in file\n\n### 7.2 Involuntary Termination\n\n**Termination for Cause:**\n- Due to misconduct or policy violation\n- May follow progressive discipline or be immediate\n- Final paycheck includes earned wages only (no PTO payout in most cases)\n- Not eligible for rehire\n\n**Termination for Performance:**\n- Failure to meet performance standards\n- Typically follows Performance Improvement Plan\n- May include PTO payout per policy\n- Rehire eligibility determined case-by-case\n\n**Layoff/Reduction in Force:**\n- Business-driven (not performance or conduct)\n- May include severance if eligible\n- Includes PTO payout per policy\n- Eligible for rehire\n\n### 7.3 Termination Process\n\n**Manager Responsibilities:**\n1. Consult with HR before termination decision\n2. Review documentation supporting termination\n3. Prepare termination letter\n4. Schedule termination meeting (recommend day other than Friday)\n5. Arrange for witness (HR representative)\n6. Plan logistics (return of property, system access, final pay)\n\n**Termination Meeting:**\n- Keep meeting brief (10-15 minutes)\n- State decision clearly and directly\n- Provide brief reason (already documented)\n- Do not argue or negotiate\n- Explain final paycheck and benefits\n- Collect Company property (keys, badge, laptop, phone)\n- Outline next steps\n- Escort from building if necessary\n\n**What to Say:**\n\"We've asked you to meet with us today to inform you that your employment with Weaver Enterprises is ending, effective today. This decision is based on [brief reason - e.g., the attendance issues we discussed in your prior written warnings]. This decision is final. [HR Representative] will explain your final paycheck, benefits continuation, and return of Company property.\"\n\n**What Not to Say:**\n- Don't apologize (undermines decision)\n- Don't go into extensive detail or debate\n- Don't compare to other employees\n- Don't make promises about references\n- Don't provide advice on unemployment benefits\n\n### 7.4 Final Pay and Benefits\n\n**Final Paycheck:**\n- Due on last day (or next regular payday, per state law)\n- Includes all earned wages through termination date\n- PTO payout per Leave Policy\n- Deductions for unreturned property allowed in some states\n\n**Benefits Continuation:**\n- Health insurance ends last day of month of termination\n- COBRA notice mailed within 14 days\n- 401(k) account remains (employee can rollover or maintain)\n- Life insurance and other benefits end per plan terms\n\n**Return of Property:**\n- Laptop, phone, tablet\n- Keys, access cards, badges\n- Credit cards\n- Company files and documents\n- Any other Company property\n\n## 8. EMPLOYEE RIGHTS AND RESPONSIBILITIES\n\n### 8.1 Right to Respond\n\nEmployees have the right to:\n- Provide their side of the story\n- Present relevant information or evidence\n- Explain circumstances or context\n- Add written comments to disciplinary documentation\n- Request HR involvement\n\n### 8.2 Right to Representation\n\n- Employees may request HR presence at disciplinary meetings\n- Union employees may request union representative (if applicable)\n- No right to attorney at Company disciplinary meetings\n\n### 8.3 Appeal Process\n\nEmployees may appeal disciplinary actions:\n\n**Who Can Appeal:**\n- Any employee receiving written warning or higher\n- Within 5 business days of disciplinary action\n\n**How to Appeal:**\n- Submit written appeal to HR\n- State reason for appeal and supporting information\n- Provide any additional evidence\n\n**Appeal Review:**\n- HR and senior leader review appeal\n- May interview parties again\n- Decision within 10 business days\n- Appeal decision is final\n\n**Possible Outcomes:**\n- Uphold discipline\n- Reduce discipline\n- Remove discipline\n- Request additional investigation\n\n### 8.4 Record Retention\n\n**Active Warnings:**\n- Verbal warnings: Not in official file (manager notes only)\n- Written warnings: Remain in file for 12 months\n- Final written warnings: Remain in file for 24 months\n- Suspensions: Remain in file permanently\n- Terminations: Remain in file permanently\n\n**Removal of Warnings:**\n- After expiration period, may be removed from \"active\" file\n- Moved to separate file but retained per retention policy\n- Still considered if future violations occur during active period\n\n## 9. MANAGER RESPONSIBILITIES\n\n### 9.1 Addressing Issues Promptly\n\nManagers should:\n- Address issues as they arise\n- Not ignore or delay discipline\n- Provide real-time feedback\n- Document performance and conduct concerns\n- Escalate to HR when appropriate\n\n### 9.2 Consistency and Fairness\n\nManagers must:\n- Apply policies consistently\n- Treat similar situations similarly\n- Avoid favoritism or bias\n- Document all disciplinary actions\n- Consult HR for guidance\n\n### 9.3 Required HR Involvement\n\nManagers must consult HR before:\n- Issuing final written warning\n- Suspending employee\n- Terminating employee\n- Addressing potential harassment or discrimination\n- Situations involving legal risk\n\nHR approval required for:\n- All terminations\n- All suspensions\n- Final written warnings\n\n## 10. HR RESPONSIBILITIES\n\nHR is responsible for:\n- Providing guidance on disciplinary process\n- Ensuring legal compliance\n- Reviewing disciplinary documentation\n- Participating in serious disciplinary meetings\n- Conducting or overseeing investigations\n- Maintaining disciplinary records\n- Training managers on disciplinary procedures\n- Monitoring for consistency across organization\n- Handling appeals\n\n## 11. TRAINING AND RESOURCES\n\n### 11.1 Manager Training\n\n**Required Training:**\n- Disciplinary action procedures (3 hours)\n- Conducting difficult conversations (2 hours)\n- Legal compliance in discipline (2 hours)\n- Documenting performance and conduct (2 hours)\n\n**Ongoing Support:**\n- HR consultation on specific situations\n- Coaching on disciplinary conversations\n- Review of documentation before meetings\n\n### 11.2 Available Resources\n\n**Templates and Tools:**\n- Disciplinary action forms\n- Termination checklists\n- Documentation guidelines\n- Sample language for common violations\n\n**HR Support:**\n- Consult with HR Business Partner\n- Employee Relations team guidance\n- Legal review for complex situations\n\n## 12. QUESTIONS AND GUIDANCE\n\nFor questions about disciplinary action:\n\n**HR Business Partners:**\n- Guidance on addressing performance and conduct issues\n- Review of disciplinary documentation\n- Support during disciplinary meetings\n\n**Employee Relations Team:**\n- Complex investigations\n- Legal compliance questions\n- Termination decisions\n\n**HR Service Center:**\n- Phone: (555) 123-4567\n- Email: hr@weaverenterprises.com\n\n---\n\n**Important Reminders:**\n\n- Consult HR before taking serious disciplinary action\n- Document all disciplinary actions thoroughly\n- Focus on correcting behavior, not punishing employees\n- Treat employees with dignity and respect\n- Apply policies consistently and fairly\n- When in doubt, ask HR\n\n*This policy is subject to change. Weaver Enterprises reserves the right to modify disciplinary procedures as needed.*"}, {"name": "health_insurance_plan", "title": "HEALTH INSURANCE PLAN - SUMMARY OF BENEFITS AND COVERAGE", "content": "# UNITEDHEALTHCARE\n## HEALTH INSURANCE PLAN - SUMMARY OF BENEFITS AND COVERAGE\n\n**Insurance Company:** UnitedHealthcare  \n**Plan Name:** Select Plus PPO Silver DZHU /L41S  \n**Coverage Period:** Based on group plan year  \n**Coverage For:** Employee/Family  \n**Plan Type:** PPO\n\n---\n\n## 1. PLAN OVERVIEW\n\nThis Summary of Benefits and Coverage (SBC) document helps you choose a health plan. The SBC shows how you and the plan share the cost for covered health care services.\n\n**Note:** Information about the cost of this plan (called the premium) will be provided separately. This is only a summary. For more information about your coverage, or to get a copy of the complete terms of coverage, visit www.welcometouhc.com or call 1-800-782-3740.\n\nFor general definitions of common terms, such as allowed amount, balance billing, coinsurance, copayment, deductible, provider, or other underlined terms, see the Glossary at www.healthcare.gov/sbc-glossary/ or call 1-866-487-2365 to request a copy.\n\n---\n\n## 2. IMPORTANT PLAN DETAILS\n\n### 2.1 Overall Deductible\n\n**Network Providers:**\n- Individual: $2,550 per calendar year\n- Family: $5,100 per calendar year\n\n**Out-of-Network Providers:**\n- Individual: $5,100 per calendar year\n- Family: $10,200 per calendar year\n\n**How It Works:** Generally, you must pay all of the costs from providers up to the deductible amount before this plan begins to pay. If you have other family members on the plan, each family member must meet their own individual deductible until the total amount of deductible expenses paid by all family members meets the overall family deductible.\n\n### 2.2 Services Covered Before You Meet Your Deductible\n\nYes. The following services are covered before you meet your deductible:\n\n- Preventive care\n- Primary care visits\n- Urgent care\n- Specialist visits\n- Outpatient services for behavioral health\n- Office visits\n- Rehabilitation services\n- Habilitation services\n- Children's eye exams\n- Children's dental check-up\n\n**Note:** This plan covers some items and services even if you haven't yet met the deductible amount. But a copayment or coinsurance may apply. For example, this plan covers certain preventive services without cost sharing and before you meet your deductible. NONDISCRIMINATION NOTICE AND ACCESS TO COMMUNICATION SERVICES\n\nUnitedHealthcare does not exclude, deny Covered Health Care Benefits to, or otherwise discriminate against any Member on the ground of race, color, national origin, ancestry, religion, sex, marital status, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, age, or disability for participation in, or receipt of the Covered Health Care Services under, any of its Health Plans, whether carried out by UnitedHealthcare directly or through a Network Medical Group or any other entity with which UnitedHealthcare arranges to carry out Covered Health Care Services under any of its Health Plans.\n\n### 5.1 Free Communication Services\n\nFree services are available to help you communicate with us such as:\n- Letters in other languages\n- Large print formats\n- Interpreter services at no charge\n\nTo ask for help, please call the toll-free number listed on your health plan ID card.\n\n### 5.2 Filing a Complaint\n\nIf you think you weren't treated fairly because of your sex, age, race, color, national origin, or disability, you can send a complaint to:\n\n**Online:** UHC Civil Rights@uhc.com\n\n**Mail:**  \nCivil Rights Coordinator  \nUnitedHealthcare Civil Rights Grievance  \nP.O. For complete terms of coverage, refer to your plan documents or contact UnitedHealthcare\n- Premium information is provided separately\n- Coverage details may vary based on your specific plan and group\n- Always verify coverage and network status before receiving services\n- Keep your health plan ID card with you at all times\n\n---\n\n**Document Reference:** SBCCA14DZHU\n\n"}, {"name": "hrbp_escalation_matrix", "title": "HRBP Escalation Matrix", "content": "# Amazon People Management\n## HRBP Escalation Matrix\n\nUse this matrix to decide who owns a case and when to escalate.\n\n### Ownership Rules (Default)\n- HRBP owns: routine people-management support, performance management guidance, promotions, compensation cycles, leave coordination, onboarding/offboarding.\n- Employee Relations (ER) owns: investigations, misconduct, harassment/discrimination claims, retaliation claims, high-risk terminations, grievances.\n- Legal owns: litigation holds, formal legal complaints, subpoenas, WARN/mass layoff compliance, contract disputes with material risk.\n- Benefits & Payroll owns: benefits eligibility/enrollment issues, COBRA, payroll corrections, deductions, wage garnishments.\n\n### Escalation Triggers\n- Escalate to ER if:\n  - any allegation of harassment/discrimination/retaliation\n  - any investigation is requested or required\n  - any termination involves misconduct or policy violations\n  - a grievance is filed or threatened\n- Escalate to Legal if:\n  - employee mentions attorney/lawsuit/regulator\n  - WARN Act thresholds may be implicated\n  - cross-border employment law is involved\n  - a severance agreement is requested or negotiated\n\n### Approval Authority (Simplified)\n- Routine HRIS updates: HRBP\n- Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) initiation: Manager + HRBP\n- Termination (performance): Manager + HRBP, ER consulted if sensitive\n- Termination (misconduct): ER required, Legal if high risk\n- Compensation exceptions (above band midpoint/max): HRBP + Comp/Finance per comp policy\n- Benefits exceptions: Benefits & Payroll Director\n\n"}, {"name": "interview_process", "title": "INTERVIEW PROCESS GUIDELINES", "content": "# AMAZON INC.\n## INTERVIEW PROCESS GUIDELINES\n\n**Document Number:** Task 102  \n**Effective Date:** January 1, 2024  \n**Last Revised:** January 1, 2024  \n**Policy Owner:** Human Resources Department  \n**Approved By:** Chief Human Resources Officer\n\n---\n\n## 1. PURPOSE AND OVERVIEW\n\nThis document provides practical guidance for conducting effective, legal, and consistent interviews at Amazon Inc. These guidelines help hiring managers and interview team members:\n\n- Conduct structured, competency-based interviews\n- Evaluate candidates fairly and objectively\n- Comply with employment laws\n- Create positive candidate experiences\n- Make informed hiring decisions\n\nAll employees participating in interviews must review and follow these guidelines.\n\n## 2. INTERVIEW PREPARATION\n\n### 2.1 Before the Interview\n\n**Review Materials:**\n- Read candidate's resume and application thoroughly\n- Review job description and key requirements\n- Understand the role's essential functions\n- Review any assessments or work samples submitted\n\n**Prepare Questions:**\n- Develop 5-8 core questions focused on key competencies\n- Use behavioral questions (\"Tell me about a time when...\")\n- Prepare follow-up probes\n- Coordinate with other interviewers to avoid duplication\n\n**Logistics:**\n- Confirm interview time and location\n- Reserve conference room or set up video call\n- Allocate sufficient time (typically 45-60 minutes)\n- Plan to arrive 5 minutes early\n- Have water available for in-person interviews\n\n**Know What You're Evaluating:**\n- Identify 3-4 key competencies for the role\n- Understand must-have vs. nice-to-have qualifications\n- Clarify decision criteria with hiring manager\n- Review compensation range\n\n### 2.2 Creating a Welcoming Environment\n\n**In-Person Interviews:**\n- Greet candidate warmly\n- Offer water or coffee\n- Provide overview of interview format\n- Put candidate at ease with brief small talk\n\n**Virtual Interviews:**\n- Test technology in advance\n- Send calendar invite with video link\n- Join video call 2-3 minutes early\n- Ensure professional background and lighting\n- Minimize distractions\n\n## 3. INTERVIEW STRUCTURE\n\n### 3.1 Recommended Interview Flow\n\n**Opening (5 minutes):**\n- Welcome and introductions\n- Brief overview of interview format and timing\n- Ask if candidate has any questions before starting\n\n**Experience and Background (10-15 minutes):**\n- Walk through resume and career progression\n- Clarify dates, roles, and responsibilities\n- Understand career motivations and transitions\n\n**Competency-Based Questions (25-30 minutes):**\n- Ask prepared behavioral questions\n- Use STAR method to evaluate responses\n- Probe for depth and specifics\n- Take notes on responses\n\n**Role and Company Discussion (5-10 minutes):**\n- Describe the position and expectations\n- Explain team structure and reporting relationships\n- Share information about company culture and values\n- Discuss growth opportunities\n\n**Candidate Questions (5-10 minutes):**\n- Give candidate time to ask questions\n- Answer honestly and thoroughly\n- Note quality and depth of questions asked\n\n**Closing (2-3 minutes):**\n- Explain next steps and timeline\n- Provide contact information\n- Thank candidate for their time\n\n### 3.2 Interview Timing\n\n**Typical Interview Lengths:**\n- Phone screen: 20-30 minutes\n- First-round interview: 45-60 minutes\n- Second-round interview: 60-90 minutes (may include multiple interviewers)\n- Final interview: 30-60 minutes\n- Panel interview: 60-90 minutes\n\nStart and end on time. If running over, ask candidate if they can stay longer.\n\n## 4. BEHAVIORAL INTERVIEWING TECHNIQUES\n\n### 4.1 What is Behavioral Interviewing\n\nBehavioral interviewing is based on the principle that past behavior is the best predictor of future performance. Instead of hypothetical questions, ask candidates to describe specific situations from their experience.\n\n**Traditional Question:**\n\"How would you handle a difficult customer?\"\n\n**Behavioral Question:**\n\"Tell me about a time when you dealt with a difficult customer. What was the situation and how did you handle it?\"\n\n### 4.2 The STAR Method\n\nEvaluate responses using the STAR framework:\n\n**S - Situation:**\n- What was the context?\n- When and where did this occur?\n- Who was involved?\n\n**T - Task:**\n- What was the challenge or goal?\n- What was the candidate's role?\n- What needed to be accomplished?\n\n**A - Action:**\n- What specific actions did the candidate take?\n- What was their thought process?\n- How did they approach the problem?\n\n**R - Result:**\n- What was the outcome?\n- What did they learn?\n- What would they do differently?\n- Were there measurable results?\n\n### 4.3 Sample Behavioral Questions by Competency\n\n**Problem Solving:**\n- \"Describe a complex problem you faced at work. How did you approach solving it?\"\n- \"Tell me about a time when you had to make a decision without having all the information you needed.\"\n\n**Teamwork and Collaboration:**\n- \"Give me an example of a successful team project you contributed to. What was your role?\"\n- \"Describe a time when you had to work with a difficult team member. How did you handle it?\"\n\n**Leadership:**\n- \"Tell me about a time when you had to motivate a team during a challenging period.\"\n- \"Describe a situation where you had to give difficult feedback to someone.\"\n\n**Adaptability:**\n- \"Describe a time when you had to quickly adapt to a significant change at work.\"\n- \"Tell me about a project where the requirements or direction changed midstream.\"\n\n**Communication:**\n- \"Give me an example of a time when you had to explain something complex to someone unfamiliar with the topic.\"\n- \"Describe a situation where you had to influence someone without direct authority.\"\n\n**Initiative:**\n- \"Tell me about a time when you identified a problem and took action without being asked.\"\n- \"Describe a project or idea you initiated on your own.\"\n\n**Time Management:**\n- \"Describe a situation where you had multiple deadlines competing for your attention. How did you prioritize?\"\n- \"Tell me about a time when you had to complete a project with a tight deadline.\"\n\n**Conflict Resolution:**\n- \"Describe a disagreement you had with a coworker. How did you resolve it?\"\n- \"Tell me about a time when you had to mediate between two people who disagreed.\"\n\n### 4.4 Probing Questions\n\nIf a candidate's initial response lacks detail, use probing questions:\n\n- \"Can you tell me more about that?\"\n- \"What specifically did you do?\"\n- \"What was your exact role in that situation?\"\n- \"What were you thinking at that moment?\"\n- \"How did others react?\"\n- \"What was the outcome?\"\n- \"What would you do differently next time?\"\n\nDon't accept vague or generalized answers. Dig deeper to understand the candidate's actual contribution and decision-making.\n\n### 4.5 Red Flags in Responses\n\n**Watch for:**\n- Vague answers without specifics\n- Speaking in \"we\" instead of \"I\" (unable to articulate personal contribution)\n- Cannot provide concrete examples\n- Blames others without taking accountability\n- Inconsistencies in story\n- Exaggerates or embellishes\n- Negative attitude about former employers or colleagues\n\n## 5. LEGAL COMPLIANCE\n\n### 5.1 Prohibited Questions\n\n**Never Ask About:**\n- Age or date of birth\n- Race, ethnicity, or national origin\n- Religion or religious observances\n- Marital status, pregnancy, or family plans\n- Sexual orientation or gender identity\n- Disabilities or medical conditions\n- Genetic information\n- Arrest records (conviction records are permissible if job-related)\n- Military discharge status\n- Citizenship (may ask about work authorization)\n- Financial status (unless directly job-related for financial positions)\n\n### 5.2 Acceptable vs. Unacceptable Questions\n\n| Topic | UNACCEPTABLE | ACCEPTABLE |\n|-------|-------------|------------|\n| Age | \"How old are you?\" | \"Are you over 18?\" (if required for job) |\n| Family | \"Do you have children?\" \"Are you planning to have children?\" | \"Are you able to work required hours/travel?\" (ask all candidates) |\n| Disability | \"Do you have any disabilities?\" | \"Can you perform the essential functions of this job with or without accommodation?\" |\n| National Origin | \"Where were you born?\" \"What's your native language?\" | \"Are you authorized to work in the United States?\" |\n| Religion | \"What religion do you practice?\" \"What holidays do you observe?\" | \"Are you available to work [specific schedule/days]?\" (ask all candidates) |\n| Arrest Record | \"Have you ever been arrested?\" | \"Have you been convicted of [specific crime relevant to job]?\" |\n| Military | \"What type of discharge did you receive?\" | \"What type of training/experience did you gain in the military?\" |\n\n### 5.3 Discussing Accommodations\n\nYou **may** ask: \"Can you perform the essential functions of this job with or without reasonable accommodation?\"\n\nIf candidate discloses a need for accommodation:\n- Discuss what accommodations might be needed\n- Engage in interactive process\n- Do not ask about nature or severity of disability\n- Do not ask about medical history\n- Refer to HR if questions arise\n\n### 5.4 Work Authorization\n\nYou **may** ask: \"Are you authorized to work in the United States?\"\n\nYou **may not** ask:\n- \"Are you a U.S. citizen?\"\n- \"Where were you born?\"\n- \"What is your immigration status?\"\n\nAll candidates must complete Form I-9 after hire (not during interview).\n\n## 6. EVALUATION AND DECISION MAKING\n\n### 6.1 Note-Taking\n\n**Do:**\n- Take notes during interview\n- Record specific examples and responses\n- Note both strengths and concerns\n- Document relevant job-related observations\n\n**Don't:**\n- Write notes about protected characteristics\n- Record subjective judgments unrelated to job (\"too old,\" \"won't fit in\")\n- Make comments about appearance, accent, or personal characteristics\n- Write anything you wouldn't want a lawyer to read in court\n\n**Remember:** Your notes may be requested in legal proceedings. Keep them professional and job-focused.\n\n### 6.2 Evaluation Criteria\n\n**Rate candidates on:**\n- Technical skills and qualifications\n- Relevant experience\n- Cultural fit and values alignment\n- Communication skills\n- Problem-solving ability\n- Specific competencies required for role\n\n**Use standardized rating scale:**\n- 1 - Does not meet requirements\n- 2 - Partially meets requirements\n- 3 - Meets requirements\n- 4 - Exceeds requirements\n- 5 - Far exceeds requirements\n\n### 6.3 Completing Interview Feedback\n\n**Submit feedback within 24 hours:**\n- Complete evaluation form in applicant tracking system\n- Provide ratings for each competency area\n- Include specific examples supporting ratings\n- Note any concerns or red flags\n- Recommend: advance, reject, or need more information\n\n**Feedback should be:**\n- Specific and evidence-based\n- Job-related\n- Objective\n- Balanced (strengths and weaknesses)\n- Free from bias or stereotypes\n\n### 6.4 Avoiding Bias\n\n**Common Biases to Avoid:**\n\n**Halo/Horn Effect:**\n- Don't let one positive trait overshadow weaknesses (halo)\n- Don't let one negative trait overshadow strengths (horn)\n\n**Similar-to-Me Bias:**\n- Don't favor candidates who share your background, interests, or experiences\n- Focus on job requirements, not personal similarities\n\n**First Impression Bias:**\n- Don't make snap judgments in first 5 minutes\n- Give candidates fair opportunity to demonstrate qualifications\n\n**Contrast Effect:**\n- Don't compare candidates to each other\n- Evaluate each against job requirements independently\n\n**Confirmation Bias:**\n- Don't look only for information confirming initial opinion\n- Remain open to evidence contradicting first impression\n\n**Recency Bias:**\n- Don't give more weight to most recent interviews\n- Review notes from all candidates when making decision\n\n**Strategies to Reduce Bias:**\n- Use structured interviews with same questions for all candidates\n- Take detailed notes rather than relying on memory\n- Use diverse interview panels\n- Focus on job-related competencies\n- Make decisions based on evidence, not gut feeling\n\n## 7. SPECIAL INTERVIEW SITUATIONS\n\n### 7.1 Panel Interviews\n\n**Benefits:**\n- Multiple perspectives on candidate\n- More efficient than sequential interviews\n- Reduces individual bias\n\n**Best Practices:**\n- Designate lead interviewer to facilitate\n- Assign questions to specific panelists in advance\n- Avoid talking over each other\n- Give candidate time to respond fully\n- Debrief immediately after interview\n\n### 7.2 Virtual Interviews\n\n**Preparation:**\n- Test technology (camera, microphone, internet)\n- Use professional background or blur feature\n- Ensure good lighting\n- Minimize background noise and distractions\n- Have backup plan (phone number) if tech fails\n\n**During Interview:**\n- Make eye contact by looking at camera\n- Speak clearly and at moderate pace\n- Be mindful of lag time\n- Watch for visual cues that candidate wants to speak\n- Use screen sharing if reviewing documents or portfolio\n\n**Considerations:**\n- Some candidates less comfortable with video\n- May be technical difficulties beyond candidate's control\n- Harder to build rapport virtually\n- Give grace for minor technical issues\n\n### 7.3 Phone Screenings\n\n**Purpose:**\n- Initial screening before investing time in full interview\n- Assess basic qualifications and interest\n- Clarify resume questions\n- Gauge communication skills\n\n**Structure:**\n- 15-30 minutes maximum\n- Focus on key qualifications\n- Explain role and expectations\n- Discuss salary range and availability\n- Determine if candidate should advance\n\n**Tips:**\n- Eliminate distractions\n- Have resume and job description in front of you\n- Take notes\n- Listen actively\n- Be prepared to sell opportunity if strong candidate\n\n### 7.4 Interviewing Internal Candidates\n\n**Special Considerations:**\n- May have more knowledge of company/role\n- Evaluate objectively against external candidates\n- Consider current performance and potential\n- Discuss transition timeline if selected\n- Handle rejection sensitively to preserve relationship\n\n**Don't:**\n- Assume you know internal candidate's capabilities\n- Give internal candidate unfair advantage\n- Skip standard interview process\n- Make promises about outcome\n\n### 7.5 Interviewing Former Employees\n\n**What to Consider:**\n- Reason for previous departure\n- Performance in previous role\n- Time since departure\n- Changes in person or company\n- Manager's perspective on return\n\n**Questions to Ask:**\n- \"Why are you interested in returning to Amazon?\"\n- \"What did you learn from your time away?\"\n- \"What would you do differently this time?\"\n- \"What attracted you to this specific role?\"\n\n### 7.6 Campus and Entry-Level Recruiting\n\n**Adjust Approach:**\n- Focus on potential over experience\n- Ask about coursework, projects, internships\n- Evaluate learning agility and enthusiasm\n- Discuss career development opportunities\n- Provide more information about company and role\n\n**Questions for New Graduates:**\n- \"Describe a challenging academic project. How did you approach it?\"\n- \"Tell me about a time you balanced multiple priorities (school, work, activities).\"\n- \"What have you learned from your internship experiences?\"\n- \"Why are you interested in this field/industry?\"\n\n## 8. SELLING THE OPPORTUNITY\n\n### 8.1 Why Selling Matters\n\nStrong candidates often have multiple offers. Part of your role is to:\n- Generate excitement about the opportunity\n- Showcase company culture and values\n- Explain growth and development possibilities\n- Build relationship with candidate\n\n### 8.2 What to Highlight\n\n**About the Role:**\n- Impactful work and key responsibilities\n- Opportunities for growth and advancement\n- Learning and development support\n- Team dynamics and collaboration\n\n**About the Company:**\n- Mission and values\n- Market position and growth trajectory\n- Innovation and industry leadership\n- Culture and employee engagement\n- Work-life balance and flexibility\n- Awards and recognition\n\n**About the Team:**\n- Team structure and dynamics\n- Collaborative environment\n- Diversity of backgrounds and perspectives\n- Support and mentorship\n\n### 8.3 Answering Candidate Questions Honestly\n\n**Be Truthful About:**\n- Challenges of the role\n- Areas where company is still developing\n- Work pace and demands\n- Why position is open\n\n**Don't:**\n- Overpromise on advancement or compensation\n- Misrepresent culture or expectations\n- Dismiss legitimate concerns\n- Provide inaccurate information\n\nSetting accurate expectations leads to better long-term fit and retention.\n\n## 9. AFTER THE INTERVIEW\n\n### 9.1 Debrief Meeting\n\n**When to Debrief:**\n- Immediately after interview while impressions are fresh\n- Within 24 hours maximum\n\n**What to Discuss:**\n- Overall impressions\n- Specific strengths and concerns\n- Evidence for and against hiring\n- Comparison to job requirements\n- Cultural fit assessment\n- Recommendation (advance, reject, need more info)\n\n**Decision-Making:**\n- Hiring manager makes final decision\n- Considers all feedback from interview team\n- HR provides guidance on process and compliance\n- Hiring manager notifies HR of decision\n\n### 9.2 Documenting Decisions\n\n**In Applicant Tracking System:**\n- Record all feedback and ratings\n- Document reasons for advancing or rejecting\n- Note any concerns that need further exploration\n- Keep decisions job-related and objective\n\n**Audit Trail:**\n- System maintains history of decisions\n- Ensures accountability and consistency\n- Supports defense if hiring decision questioned\n\n### 9.3 Reference Checks\n\nIf moving forward with candidate:\n- Conduct reference checks before offer\n- Ask structured questions about performance\n- Listen for what's not said\n- Verify employment dates and titles\n- Ask about rehire eligibility\n\n## 10. COMMON INTERVIEW MISTAKES\n\n### 10.1 Mistakes to Avoid\n\n**Talking Too Much:**\n- Interview should be 70% candidate talking, 30% interviewer\n- Don't monopolize conversation\n- Give candidate time to fully answer\n\n**Leading Questions:**\n- Don't suggest desired answer\n- Bad: \"We value teamwork here. Are you a team player?\"\n- Good: \"Describe your experience working in teams.\"\n\n**Not Listening:**\n- Don't mentally prepare next question while candidate speaks\n- Focus on what candidate is actually saying\n- Take notes to help remember details\n\n**Making Snap Judgments:**\n- Don't decide in first 5 minutes\n- Give candidate full interview to demonstrate qualifications\n- Evaluate based on entire interview, not first impression\n\n**Forgetting to Sell:**\n- Don't treat interview as interrogation\n- Balance evaluation with selling opportunity\n- Show enthusiasm for role and company\n\n**Not Preparing:**\n- Don't wing it\n- Review materials and prepare questions\n- Understand role requirements\n\n**Asking Hypothetical Questions:**\n- Don't ask \"What would you do if...\"\n- Ask for actual examples: \"Tell me about a time when...\"\n\n**Allowing Interruptions:**\n- Don't check phone or email during interview\n- Give candidate full attention\n- Close door and minimize distractions\n\n## 11. CANDIDATE REJECTION GUIDELINES\n\n### 11.1 When to Reject Candidates\n\n**Post-Application Review:**\n- Does not meet minimum qualifications\n- Resume shows significant unexplained gaps\n- Application contains false information\n- Compensation expectations far exceed budget\n\n**Post-Phone Screen:**\n- Basic qualifications not as strong as indicated on resume\n- Poor communication skills for role requirements\n- Lack of genuine interest in position\n- Availability doesn't match needs\n\n**Post-Interview:**\n- Insufficient experience or skills\n- Poor cultural fit\n- Negative references\n- Better qualified candidates in pipeline\n- Concerns about reliability or professionalism\n\n**Post-Background Check:**\n- Failed background check (see Recruitment & Hiring Policy)\n- Failed drug test (for applicable positions)\n- Unable to verify credentials\n- Discrepancies in employment history\n\n### 11.2 Timing of Rejection Communication\n\n**Prompt Notification:**\n- Post-resume review: Within 2 weeks of application\n- Post-phone screen: Within 1 week\n- Post-first interview: Within 1-2 weeks\n- Post-final interview: Within 1 week\n\n**Why Timely Communication Matters:**\n- Shows respect for candidate's time\n- Maintains positive employer brand\n- Allows candidates to pursue other opportunities\n- Reflects professional organization\n\n### 11.3 Methods of Communication\n\n**Email Rejection (Appropriate for):**\n- Early-stage candidates (application review, phone screen)\n- High-volume recruiting situations\n- When candidate relationship is minimal\n\n**Phone Call Rejection (Recommended for):**\n- Final-round candidates\n- Internal candidates\n- When lengthy interview process\n- When candidate asked for feedback\n- When candidate was a strong contender\n\n**In-Person Rejection (Reserved for):**\n- Internal candidates (when appropriate)\n- When candidate is well-known to organization\n- Executive-level positions\n\n### 11.4 What to Say in Rejection Communications\n\n**Key Messages to Include:**\n- Thank candidate for their time and interest\n- Indicate decision has been made\n- Communicate decision clearly (don't leave false hope)\n- Encourage future applications if appropriate\n- Maintain professional, respectful tone\n\n**Email Rejection Template - Early Stage:**\n\n*Subject: Update on Your Application - [Position Title]*\n\nDear [Candidate Name],\n\nThank you for your interest in the [Position Title] role at Amazon and for taking the time to apply.\n\nAfter careful review of your application, we have decided to move forward with other candidates whose qualifications more closely match our current needs for this position.\n\nWe appreciate your interest in Amazon and encourage you to apply for future You can view our current openings at [careers website].\n\nWe wish you the best in your job search.\n\nSincerely,\n[Name]\nTalent Acquisition Team\n\n---\n\n**Email Rejection Template - Post-Interview:**\n\n*Subject: [Position Title] Interview Follow-Up*\n\nDear [Candidate Name],\n\nThank you for taking the time to interview for the [Position Title] position and for your interest in joining Amazon. We enjoyed learning about your experience and appreciated the opportunity to meet with you.\n\nAfter careful consideration, we have decided to move forward with another candidate whose background and experience more closely align with the specific requirements of this role.\n\nThis was a competitive process with many qualified candidates, and we recognize the time and effort you invested in interviewing with us. We were impressed by [specific positive element from interview] and encourage you to apply for future opportunities that match your skills and experience.\n\nThank you again for your interest in Amazon. We wish you continued success in your career.\n\nBest regards,\n[Hiring Manager Name]\n\n---\n\n**Phone Call Rejection Script:**\n\n\"Hi [Candidate Name], this is [Your Name] from Amazon. Do you have a few minutes to talk?\n\nI wanted to reach out personally to let you know that we've made a decision on the [Position Title] role. Your [specific strength] really stood out, and we were impressed by [specific positive element]. Ultimately, we selected a candidate whose experience in [specific area] more closely matched our immediate needs.\n\nI want to thank you for the time you invested in our process and for your interest in Amazon. [If appropriate: I'd encourage you to keep an eye on our careers page for future opportunities that might be a fit.]\n\nDo you have any questions for me?\"\n\n### 11.5 Providing Feedback\n\n**General Guidelines:**\n- Not legally required to provide feedback\n- Use caution when providing detailed feedback\n- Keep feedback high-level and constructive\n- Avoid statements that could be legally problematic\n\n**When to Provide Feedback:**\n- Final-round candidates who request it\n- Internal candidates (with appropriate care)\n- When there's a developmental relationship\n\n**When to Decline Feedback:**\n- Legal concerns about discrimination claims\n- Concerns candidate will argue or become hostile\n- No clear, objective basis for decision\n- Company policy prohibits (consult HR)\n\n**How to Provide Constructive Feedback:**\n\n**Do:**\n- Focus on qualifications and fit, not personality\n- Provide specific, job-related observations\n- Frame as developmental areas\n- Suggest areas for growth\n- Keep it brief (2-3 key points maximum)\n\n**Don't:**\n- Provide exhaustive list of deficiencies\n- Make it personal or attack character\n- Compare to other candidates\n- Discuss protected characteristics\n- Put anything in writing that could be misinterpreted\n\n**Sample Feedback Statements:**\n\n\u2705 GOOD:\n- \"We were looking for someone with more direct experience managing enterprise-level implementations.\"\n- \"The role requires extensive public speaking, and we selected a candidate with a stronger presentation background.\"\n- \"We needed someone who could start immediately, and another candidate had better availability.\"\n\n\u274c AVOID:\n- \"You're not a good cultural fit.\" (Too vague, could imply bias)\n- \"You seemed nervous and not confident enough.\" (Subjective, could relate to disability)\n- \"We went with someone younger and more energetic.\" (Age discrimination)\n- \"We needed someone more technical.\" (If said to a woman in tech, could imply gender bias)\n\n### 11.6 Handling Difficult Rejection Conversations\n\n**If Candidate Becomes Emotional:**\n- Remain calm and empathetic\n- Allow them to express feelings\n- Don't argue or become defensive\n- Reiterate appreciation for their interest\n- End conversation professionally if becomes inappropriate\n\n**If Candidate Argues or Challenges Decision:**\n- Remain professional and firm\n- Restate that decision is final\n- Don't provide additional detail or justification\n- Don't be drawn into debate\n- End call if necessary: \"I understand you're disappointed, but the decision is final. I wish you the best in your search.\"\n\n**If Candidate Threatens Legal Action:**\n- Remain calm and professional\n- Don't admit fault or apologize excessively\n- Document conversation immediately\n- Report to HR and Legal immediately\n- Do not continue to engage with candidate\n\n**If Candidate Asks About Discrimination:**\n- Take concern seriously\n- Document specific allegations\n- Refer to HR immediately\n- Don't dismiss or argue\n- Don't provide detailed defense\n\n### 11.7 Rejection of Internal Candidates\n\n**Special Considerations:**\n- More sensitive due to ongoing employment relationship\n- Risk of disengagement or resignation\n- May impact team morale\n- Requires thoughtful communication\n\n**Best Practices:**\n\n**Timing:**\n- Notify before external candidates\n- Don't let them hear through grapevine\n- Allow time to process before announcement\n\n**Communication:**\n- Phone call or in-person (not email)\n- Private setting\n- Sufficient time for discussion\n- Involve current manager if appropriate\n\n**Message:**\n- Be direct but compassionate\n- Acknowledge their strengths and contributions\n- Explain decision objectively\n- Discuss development opportunities\n- Reaffirm value to organization in current role\n- Discuss path forward\n\n**Follow-Up:**\n- Check in within a week\n- Offer development planning\n- Discuss future opportunities\n- Monitor engagement and morale\n\n**Sample Internal Rejection Script:**\n\n\"[Name], I wanted to talk with you about the [Position Title] role you interviewed for. First, I want to thank you for putting yourself forward and for the time you invested in the process.\n\nAfter careful consideration, we've decided to move forward with another candidate. This was a difficult decision because you have [specific strengths]. Ultimately, we selected someone with [specific differentiator relevant to role].\n\nI want to be clear that this doesn't diminish the value you bring in your current role. Your work on [specific contributions] has been excellent, and you're an important part of the team.\n\nI'd like to discuss how we can support your development and career growth here at Amazon. Are you open to having that conversation?\"\n\n### 11.8 Maintaining Candidate Relationships\n\n**Why It Matters:**\n- Future job openings may be better fit\n- Candidate may refer others\n- Candidate may become customer or partner\n- Protects employer brand and reputation\n\n**Silver Medal Program:**\n- Keep strong candidates warm for future openings\n- Add to talent pipeline or talent community\n- Notify of relevant future opportunities\n- Maintain relationship through periodic touchpoints\n\n**Candidate Reapplication:**\n- Welcome qualified candidates to reapply\n- Review with fresh perspective\n- Don't hold past rejection against them\n- Consider growth since last application\n\n### 11.9 Legal Considerations\n\n**Document Everything:**\n- Reason for rejection must be job-related\n- Document in applicant tracking system\n- Keep notes professional and objective\n- Consistent treatment across candidates\n\n**Avoid Discriminatory Reasons:**\n- Never reject based on protected characteristics\n- Ensure reason is legitimate and non-discriminatory\n- Apply same standards to all candidates\n- Be prepared to defend decision if challenged\n\n**Retain Records:**\n- Keep all application materials per retention policy\n- Maintain interview notes and evaluations\n- Document communication with candidate\n- Store securely and confidentially\n\n**Red Flags Requiring HR/Legal Review:**\n- Candidate threatens legal action\n- Decision could appear discriminatory\n- Internal candidate with performance issues\n- High-profile or executive-level rejection\n- Unusual circumstances or complications\n\n## 12. TRAINING AND RESOURCES\n\n### 12.1 Required Training\n\nAll hiring managers and frequent interviewers must complete:\n- Interview skills training (4 hours)\n- Legal compliance in hiring (2 hours)\n- Unconscious bias awareness (2 hours)\n- Candidate communication and rejection best practices (1 hour)\n- Annual refresher training\n\n### 12.2 Available Resources\n\n**Interview Question Bank:**\n- Organized by competency\n- Behavioral and situational questions\n- Follow-up probes\n- Access via HR portal\n\n**Interview Evaluation Templates:**\n- Standardized rating forms\n- Competency scorecards\n- Reference check forms\n\n**Rejection Communication Templates:**\n- Email templates for various stages\n- Phone script templates\n- Internal candidate rejection guidance\n\n**Support from HR:**\n- Consult with HR Business Partner or Recruiting team\n- Questions about process or compliance\n- Coaching on interview techniques\n- Debriefing on difficult interviews\n- Guidance on candidate rejections and feedback\n\n## 13.
